questions.question: Define an array and explain its key characteristics.
keyPoints: Collection of elements of same data type
Stored in contiguous memory locations
Elements accessed using indices
Provides O(1) random access time
Fixed size in static arrays

questions.question: Explain the difference between static and dynamic arrays with examples.
keyPoints: Static arrays have fixed size declared at compile time (e.g., int arr[10])
Dynamic arrays can resize during runtime (e.g., ArrayList, vector, Python list)
Dynamic arrays use amortization for efficient insertions
Static arrays are more memory efficient when size is known

questions.question: What is row-major and column-major ordering in 2D arrays?
keyPoints: Row-major: elements stored row by row (C, C++, Java, Python)
Column-major: elements stored column by column (Fortran, MATLAB)
Row-major address: Base + (i*n + j)*size
Column-major address: Base + (i + j*m)*size

questions.question: Discuss the time complexity of basic array operations.
keyPoints: Access by index: O(1) - constant time
Search: O(n) - linear search required
Insert at beginning: O(n) - shifting required
Insert at end: O(1) for dynamic, O(1) for static if space available
Delete at end: O(1)
Delete at beginning: O(n) - shifting required

questions.question: What is a jagged array? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: A jagged array is a 2D array where each row can have different number of columns
Also called array of arrays
Example in Java: int[][] jagged = {{1,2}, {3,4,5}, {6,7,8,9}}
First row has 2 elements, second has 3, third has 4
More flexible than rectangular 2D arrays

questions.question: Explain the advantages and disadvantages of arrays.
keyPoints: Advantages: O(1) random access, cache-friendly, simple implementation, memory efficient for same-type elements
Disadvantages: Fixed size (static), expensive insertion/deletion in middle, wastes memory if oversized, requires contiguous memory
Trade-off between fast access and flexibility

questions.question: What is the base address of an array? How is the address of any element calculated?
keyPoints: Base address is the memory location of the first element (index 0)
Formula: Address of A[i] = Base + (i × size_of_element)
For 2D row-major: Address = Base + ((i × n) + j) × size
This direct calculation enables O(1) access time

questions.question: Compare arrays and linked lists as data structures. Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Arrays: O(1) access, O(n) insertion/deletion, contiguous memory, cache-friendly
Linked Lists: O(n) access, O(1) insertion/deletion at known position, non-contiguous memory
Choose Arrays when: random access is frequent, size is known, cache performance matters
Choose Linked Lists when: frequent insertions/deletions, size unknown, memory fragmentation concern
Hybrid approach: use both based on requirements

questions.question: Explain how dynamic arrays work. Discuss the concept of amortized analysis with reference to array resizing.
keyPoints: Dynamic arrays (ArrayList, vector, Python list) can grow and shrink
When array is full, create new array of double size (typically)
Copy all elements to new array - this is O(n) operation
Amortized analysis: occasional O(n) cost spread over many O(1) insertions
Cost per operation = O(1) amortized despite occasional O(n)
Growth factor typically 2 (doubles capacity)
Space-time tradeoff: extra capacity vs frequent resizing

questions.question: Write an algorithm to search an element in a sorted array. Explain its time complexity and compare it with linear search.
keyPoints: Binary Search: divide search space in half each iteration
Algorithm: compare target with middle element, eliminate half based on comparison
Time complexity: O(log n) - much faster than linear O(n)
Requires sorted array as prerequisite
Implementation can be iterative or recursive
For small arrays (< 20 elements), linear search may be faster due to less overhead
Practical considerations: binary search needs sorted data, linear search doesn't
